chapters.title: Functions
chapters.summary: Introduces functions, their graphs, combinations, transformations, and trigonometric functions.

concepts.name: Natural Domain of a Function
concepts.description: The largest set of real numbers for which the function formula gives real, defined output values.

questions.text: Find the natural domain and graph the function f(x) = 5 - 2x.
questions.answer_hint: Linear function defined for all real x.

questions.text: Find the natural domain and graph the function f(x) = 1 - 2x - x^2.
questions.answer_hint: Quadratic function defined for all real x.

questions.text: Find the natural domain and graph g(x) = 2|x|.
questions.answer_hint: Absolute value function defined for all real x.

questions.text: Find the natural domain and graph g(x) = 2^(-x).
questions.answer_hint: Exponential function defined for all real x.

questions.text: Find the natural domain and graph F(t) = t/|t|.
questions.answer_hint: Defined for all t ≠ 0; equals 1 for t>0 and -1 for t<0.

questions.text: Find the natural domain and graph G(t) = 1/|t|.
questions.answer_hint: Defined for all t ≠ 0.

questions.text: Find the domain of y = (x+3)/(4 - sqrt(x^2 - 9)).
questions.answer_hint: Require x^2 - 9 >= 0 and 4 - sqrt(x^2-9) ≠ 0.

questions.text: Find the range of y = (2 + x^2)/(x^2 + 4).
questions.answer_hint: Set y = (2+x^2)/(x^2+4) and solve for x in terms of y.

concepts.name: Graphs of Functions and the Vertical Line Test
concepts.description: A curve in the xy-plane is the graph of a function of x if and only if no vertical line intersects the curve more than once.

questions.text: Graph |y| = x and explain why it is not the graph of a function of x.
questions.answer_hint: For each positive x, there are two y values (positive and negative).

questions.text: Graph y^2 = x^2 and explain why it is not the graph of a function of x.
questions.answer_hint: Gives y = ±x, two values for most x.

questions.text: Graph |x| + |y| = 1 and explain why it is not the graph of a function of x.
questions.answer_hint: Diamond shape fails vertical line test.

questions.text: Graph |x + y| = 1 and explain why it is not the graph of a function of x.
questions.answer_hint: Gives two lines, fails vertical line test.

concepts.name: Piecewise-Defined Functions
concepts.description: Functions defined by different formulas on different parts of their domain.

questions.answer_hint: Two line segments meeting at (1,1).
Two line segments; check continuity at x=1.
Parabola pieces; check continuity at x=1.

questions.text: Graph G(x) = {1/x, x<0; x, x>=0}.
questions.answer_hint: Hyperbola for x<0, ray for x>=0.

questions.text: Find a formula for each function graphed in Exercises 29-32.
questions.answer_hint: Identify slopes and intercepts from graph to write piecewise formulas.

concepts.name: Proportionality and Applied Functions
concepts.description: Using direct and inverse proportionality to model real-world relationships as functions.

questions.text: Let L be the distance between the points (x,y) and (4,0). Write L as a function of y.
questions.answer_hint: Use distance formula: L = sqrt((x-4)^2 + y^2), then express x in terms of y if on a curve.

questions.text: The kinetic energy K of a mass is proportional to the square of its velocity v. If K=12,960 joules when v=18 m/s, what is K when v=10 m/s?
questions.answer_hint: Find constant of proportionality k = K/v^2, then compute K for v=10.

questions.text: The variables r and s are inversely proportional, and r=6 when s=4. Determine s when r=10.
questions.answer_hint: rs = constant = 24; s = 24/10 = 2.4.

questions.text: Boyle's Law: If P=14.7 lb/in^2 when V=1000 in^3, what is V when P=23.4 lb/in^2?
questions.answer_hint: PV = constant; V = 14700/23.4.

questions.text: A box with open top is constructed from a 14x22 in. cardboard by cutting squares of side x at each corner. Express volume V as a function of x.
A rectangle is inscribed in an isosceles right triangle with hypotenuse 2 units. Express the y-coordinate of P in terms of x, and express the area of the rectangle in terms of x.
questions.answer_hint: Line AB has equation y = 1-x; Area = 2x(1-x).

questions.text: Three hundred books sell for $40 each. For each $5 increase in price, 25 fewer books are sold. Write revenue R as a function of the number x of $5 increases.
A pen shaped as an isosceles right triangle with legs x ft and hypotenuse h ft. Fencing costs $5/ft for legs and $10/ft for hypotenuse. Write total cost C as a function of h.
questions.answer_hint: Legs = h/sqrt(2) each; C = 2*(5*(h/sqrt(2))) + 10h.

questions.text: A power plant is next to a river 800 ft wide. A cable goes to a city 2 mi downstream on the opposite side. Cost is $180/ft across river and $100/ft along land. Write C(x) for cost in terms of x (distance from directly opposite point).

concepts.name: Symmetry and Graph Analysis
concepts.description: Understanding even/odd symmetry and x-axis symmetry in relation to functions.

questions.text: Match equations y=x^4, y=x^7, y=x^10 to their graphs without a graphing device.
questions.answer_hint: Even powers are symmetric about y-axis; odd powers are not.

questions.text: Match equations y=5x, y=5^x, y=x^5 to their graphs without a graphing device.
questions.answer_hint: Distinguish linear, exponential, and odd-power functions.

questions.text: For a curve symmetric about the x-axis, explain why it cannot be the graph of a function unless y=0.
questions.answer_hint: If (x,y) and (x,-y) both lie on the curve, the vertical line test fails unless y=0.

questions.text: Graph f(x)=x/2 and g(x)=1+(4/x) to identify values of x for which x/2 > 1+4/x, then confirm algebraically.
questions.answer_hint: Find intersection points and test intervals.

questions.text: Graph f(x)=3/(x-1) and g(x)=2/(x+1) to identify values of x for which 3/(x-1) < 2/(x+1), then confirm algebraically.
questions.answer_hint: Find intersection points and test intervals, noting domain restrictions.

sections.title: Combining Functions; Shifting and Scaling Graphs

concepts.name: Algebraic Combinations of Functions
concepts.description: Functions can be added, subtracted, multiplied, and divided to produce new functions. The domain of the combined function is the intersection of the domains of the original functions.

questions.text: Find the domains and ranges of f, g, f+g, and f*g where f(x)=x and g(x)=sqrt(x-1).
questions.answer_hint: Domain of f+g and f*g is intersection: [1, infinity).

questions.text: Find the domains and ranges of f, g, f+g, and f*g where f(x)=sqrt(x+1) and g(x)=sqrt(x-1).
questions.answer_hint: Domain of f+g is [1, infinity).

questions.text: Find the domains and ranges of f, g, f/g, and g/f where f(x)=2 and g(x)=x^2+1.
questions.answer_hint: f/g defined everywhere; g/f defined everywhere.

questions.text: Find the domains and ranges of f, g, f/g, and g/f where f(x)=1 and g(x)=1+sqrt(x).
questions.answer_hint: g/f defined for x>=0; f/g defined where g≠0.

concepts.name: Composite Functions
concepts.description: The composite function f∘g is defined by (f∘g)(x) = f(g(x)). The domain consists of numbers x in the domain of g for which g(x) lies in the domain of f.

questions.answer_hint: Substitute systematically: f(g(0))=f(-3)=2, etc.
Watch domain restrictions when composing.

questions.text: Write a formula for f∘g∘h where f(x)=x+1, g(x)=3x, h(x)=4-x.
Write a formula for f∘g∘h where f(x)=3x+4, g(x)=2x-1, h(x)=x^2.
questions.answer_hint: Compose step by step.

questions.text: Write a formula for f∘g∘h where f(x)=sqrt(x+1), g(x)=1/(x+4), h(x)=1/x.
questions.answer_hint: h(x)=1/x, g(h(x))=1/(1/x+4), then apply f.

questions.text: Write a formula for f∘g∘h where f(x)=(x+2)/(3-x), g(x)=x^2/(x^2+1), h(x)=sqrt(2-x).
questions.answer_hint: Compose step by step, tracking domain restrictions.

questions.text: Express y=sqrt(x-3) as a composite of f(x)=x-3, g(x)=sqrt(x), h(x)=x^3, j(x)=sqrt(x).
questions.answer_hint: y = g∘f.

questions.text: Copy and complete the table: given g(x) and f(x), find (f∘g)(x) or missing function.
questions.answer_hint: Work backwards from (f∘g)(x) to find missing g or f.

questions.text: Evaluate f(g(-1)), g(f(0)), f(f(-1)), g(g(2)), g(f(-2)), f(g(1)) using the given table of values.
questions.answer_hint: Read values from table and substitute.

questions.text: Evaluate composite expressions using f(x)=2-x and g(x)={-x, -2<=x<0; x-1, 0<=x<=2}.
questions.answer_hint: Determine which piece of g applies before composing.

questions.text: For f(x)=sqrt(x+1) and g(x)=1/x, write formulas for f∘g and g∘f and find domain and range of each.
questions.answer_hint: f∘g: domain x>0; g∘f: domain x>=-1, x≠-1.

questions.text: For f(x)=x^2 and g(x)=1-2x, write formulas for f∘g and g∘f and find domain and range of each.
Let f(x)=x/(x-2). Find g(x) so that (f∘g)(x)=x.
questions.answer_hint: Solve f(g(x))=x for g(x): g(x)=2x/(x-1).

questions.text: Let f(x)=2x^3-4. Find g(x) so that (f∘g)(x)=x+2.
questions.answer_hint: Solve 2(g(x))^3-4=x+2 for g(x).

concepts.name: Shifting Graphs
concepts.description: Vertical and horizontal shifts of function graphs obtained by adding constants to the output or input of a function.
key_formulas: y = f(x) + k: shifts graph up k units (k>0) or down |k| units (k<0)
y = f(x+h): shifts graph left h units (h>0) or right |h| units (h<0)

questions.text: The graph of y=-x^2 is shifted to two new positions. Write equations for the new graphs.
questions.answer_hint: Identify vertical/horizontal shifts from the figure.

questions.text: The graph of y=x^2 is shifted to two new positions. Write equations for the new graphs.
questions.answer_hint: Identify vertical/horizontal shifts from the figure.

questions.text: Match equations y=(x-1)^2-4, y=(x-2)^2+2, y=(x+2)^2+2, y=(x+3)^2-2 to the graphs.
questions.answer_hint: Vertex of each parabola identifies the position.

questions.text: The graph of y=-x^2 is shifted to four new positions with vertices at (-2,3), (1,4), (2,0), (-4,-1). Write equations.
questions.answer_hint: y = -(x-h)^2 + k for each vertex (h,k).

questions.text: Shift x^2+y^2=49 down 3 and left 2. Give the equation for the shifted graph.
Shift y=x^3 left 1 and down 1. Give the equation and sketch.
Graph y=sqrt(x+4).
questions.answer_hint: Shift y=sqrt(x) left 4 units.

questions.text: Graph y=|x-2|-1.
questions.answer_hint: Shift y=|x| right 2 and down 1.

concepts.name: Scaling and Reflecting Graphs
concepts.description: Stretching or compressing a graph vertically or horizontally by multiplying the function or its argument by a constant. Reflections are special cases with c=-1.
key_formulas: y = cf(x): stretches vertically by factor c (c>1)
y = (1/c)f(x): compresses vertically by factor c (c>1)
y = f(cx): compresses horizontally by factor c (c>1)
y = f(x/c): stretches horizontally by factor c (c>1)

questions.text: Give an equation for y=x^2-1 stretched vertically by a factor of 3.
Give an equation for y=x^2-1 compressed horizontally by a factor of 2.
Give an equation for y=1+1/x^2 compressed vertically by a factor of 2.
Give an equation for y=1+1/x^2 stretched horizontally by a factor of 3.
Give an equation for y=sqrt(4-x^2) stretched horizontally by a factor of 2.
Given f(x)=x^4-4x^3+10, find the formula for the graph compressed horizontally by factor 2 then reflected across y-axis.
Given f(x)=x^4-4x^3+10, find the formula for the graph compressed vertically by factor 2 then reflected across x-axis.

concepts.name: Even and Odd Functions
concepts.description: A function is even if f(-x)=f(x) and odd if f(-x)=-f(x). Products and compositions of even/odd functions follow specific rules.

questions.text: Assume f is even and g is odd. Determine which of fg, f/g, g/f, f^2, g^2, f∘g, g∘f, f∘f, g∘g are even or odd.
questions.answer_hint: Use definitions: (fg)(-x)=f(-x)g(-x)=f(x)(-g(x))=-(fg)(x), so fg is odd.

questions.text: Can a function be both even and odd? Give reasons.
questions.answer_hint: Yes, only f(x)=0 satisfies both conditions.

concepts.name: Radian Measure
concepts.description: Angles measured in radians where the radian measure equals the arc length divided by the radius. One full revolution = 2π radians = 360°.

concepts.name: The Six Basic Trigonometric Functions
concepts.description: Sine, cosine, tangent, cosecant, secant, and cotangent defined in terms of coordinates of a point on a circle of radius r.

concepts.name: Exact Trigonometric Values for Special Angles
concepts.description: Exact values of sin, cos, tan for angles π/6, π/4, π/3 and their multiples derived from special right triangles.

concepts.name: Periodicity of Trigonometric Functions
concepts.description: The six trigonometric functions are periodic. Sine, cosine, secant, and cosecant have period 2π; tangent and cotangent have period π.

concepts.name: Graphs of Trigonometric Functions
concepts.description: Graphs of the six basic trigonometric functions showing domain, range, and period. Sine and cosine have range [-1,1]; tangent and cotangent have range (-∞,∞); secant and cosecant have range (-∞,-1]∪[1,∞).

concepts.name: CAST Rule
concepts.description: A mnemonic for remembering the signs of trigonometric functions in each quadrant: All positive in Q1, Sine positive in Q2, Tangent positive in Q3, Cosine positive in Q4.

concepts.name: Viewing Windows and Graphing Software
concepts.description: Understanding how the choice of viewing window affects the displayed graph; square vs. non-square windows and their distortions.

questions.text: For f(x) = x⁴ − 7x² + 6x, which of the given viewing windows displays the most appropriate graph: (a) [−1,1]×[−1,1], (b) [−2,2]×[−5,5], (c) [−10,10]×[−10,10], (d) [−5,5]×[−25,15]?
questions.answer_hint: Choose the window that shows all key features including local extrema.
Look for the window showing all roots and turning points.
The cubic has a local max and min; choose window showing both.
Find the domain first: 25 + 4x − x² ≥ 0.

concepts.name: Finding Appropriate Viewing Windows
concepts.description: Selecting viewing windows that capture the overall behavior of various functions including polynomials, rational functions, and trigonometric functions.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = x⁴ − 4x³ + 15 and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Try [−2, 5] × [−10, 30] to capture the minimum.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = x³/3 − x²/2 − 2x + 1 and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Find critical points to determine appropriate range.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = x⁵ − 5x⁴ + 10 and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: The function has a local min near x = 4.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = 4x³ − x⁴ and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Roots at x = 0 and x = 4; local max between them.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = x√(9 − x²) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Domain: [−3, 3].

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = x²(6 − x³) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Root at x = 0 (double) and x = 6^(1/3).

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = 2x − 3x^(2/3) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Check behavior near x = 0 and for large x.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = x^(1/3)(x² − 8) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Roots at x = 0 and x = ±2√2.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = 5x^(2/5) − 2x and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Check for local max near x = 1.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = x^(2/3)(5 − x) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Roots at x = 0 and x = 5.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = |x² − 1| and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: V-shapes at x = ±1.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = |x² − x| and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Zeros at x = 0 and x = 1.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = (x + 3)/(x + 2) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Vertical asymptote at x = −2; horizontal asymptote at y = 1.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = 1 − 1/(x + 3) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Vertical asymptote at x = −3.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = (x² + 2)/(x² + 1) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: No vertical asymptotes; horizontal asymptote at y = 1.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = (x² − 1)/(x² + 1) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Roots at x = ±1; horizontal asymptote at y = 1.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = (x − 1)/(x² − x − 6) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Vertical asymptotes at x = 3 and x = −2.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = 8/(x² − 9) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Vertical asymptotes at x = ±3.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = (6x² − 15x + 6)/(4x² − 10x) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Factor numerator and denominator to find asymptotes and holes.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for f(x) = (x² − 3)/(x − 2) and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Vertical asymptote at x = 2; oblique asymptote.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = sin 250x and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Period = 2π/250 ≈ 0.025; use a very small window.

questions.text: Find an appropriate viewing window for y = 3 cos 60x and display its graph.
questions.answer_hint: Period = 2π/60 ≈ 0.105; use a small window.

concepts.name: Regression Analysis and Trend Curves
concepts.description: Using least squares regression to find linear or polynomial curves that capture the trend of collected data; scatterplots and regression curves.
key_formulas: Least squares minimizes sum of squared vertical distances

concepts.name: Obtaining Complete Graphs with Software
concepts.description: Addressing software limitations such as missing branches for functions with negative inputs, steep near-vertical lines at asymptotes, and rapidly oscillating functions.

concepts.name: Exponential Behavior and Definition
concepts.description: An exponential function f(x) = a^x where a > 0 and a ≠ 1. The variable appears in the exponent. Exponential functions model doubling, compound interest, and other multiplicative growth processes.

questions.text: In 2014, $100 is invested in a savings account with 5.5% interest compounded annually. Give a formula for the amount A after x years.
Sketch the curves y = 2^x, y = 4^x, y = 3^(-x), y = (1/5)^x together and label each.
questions.answer_hint: Larger base means steeper growth; negative exponent means decay.

questions.text: Sketch y = 2^(-t) and y = -2^t.
questions.answer_hint: 2^(-t) is a decay curve above x-axis; -2^t is a growth curve below x-axis.

questions.text: Sketch y = e^x and y = 1/e^x.
questions.answer_hint: 1/e^x = e^(-x), which is the reflection of e^x across the y-axis.

questions.text: Sketch the shifted exponential curves y = 2^x - 1 and y = 2^(-x) - 1.
questions.answer_hint: Shift the standard exponential curves down by 1 unit.

concepts.name: Rules for Exponents
concepts.description: Algebraic rules governing operations with exponential expressions for positive bases and all real exponents.

questions.answer_hint: Use rule 1: add exponents. 16^(2 - 1.75) = 16^0.25.

questions.text: Simplify 4^4.2 / 4^3.7.
questions.answer_hint: Subtract exponents: 4^(4.2 - 3.7) = 4^0.5 = 2.
Subtract exponents: 3^(5/3 - 2/3) = 3^1 = 3.

questions.text: Simplify 125^(1/8*4).
Simplify 2^(sqrt(3)) * 7^(sqrt(3)).
questions.answer_hint: Use rule 4: (2*7)^sqrt(3) = 14^sqrt(3).

questions.text: Simplify (2/sqrt(2))^4.
questions.answer_hint: 2/sqrt(2) = sqrt(2), so (sqrt(2))^4 = 4.

questions.text: Simplify (sqrt(6)/3)^2.
questions.answer_hint: Square numerator and denominator: 6/9 = 2/3.

concepts.name: The Natural Exponential Function e^x
concepts.description: The natural exponential function f(x) = e^x uses the irrational base e ≈ 2.71828. It is the unique exponential function whose graph has slope exactly 1 when crossing the y-axis, making it fundamental to calculus.
key_formulas: slope of y = e^x at x=0 is 1

questions.text: Find the domain and range of f(x) = 1/(2 + e^x).
questions.answer_hint: Domain: all reals; Range: (0, 1/2) since e^x > 0 always.

questions.text: Find the domain and range of g(t) = cos(e^(-t)).
questions.answer_hint: Domain: all reals; e^(-t) in (0, inf), so range is [cos values over (0,inf)].

questions.text: Find the domain and range of g(t) = sqrt(1 + 3^(-t)).
questions.answer_hint: Domain: all reals; 3^(-t) > 0 so 1 + 3^(-t) > 1, range is (1, inf).

questions.text: Find the domain and range of f(x) = 3/(1 - e^(2x)).
questions.answer_hint: Domain: all x ≠ 0; analyze sign of denominator for range.

concepts.name: Exponential Growth and Decay
concepts.description: Models of the form y = y0 * e^(kx) describe exponential growth (k > 0) or decay (k < 0). Applications include compound interest, population growth, radioactive decay, and disease spread.

questions.text: Use the model y = Pe^(rt) to track the growth of $100 invested in 2014 at 5.5% annual interest. Predict the amount after 4 years.
Carbon-14 decays with rate r = 1.2e-4 per year. Predict the percent of carbon-14 remaining after 866 years.
Use graphs to find approximate solutions to 2^x = 5.
Use graphs to find approximate solutions to e^x = 4.
Use graphs to find approximate solutions to 3^x - 0.5 = 0.
Use graphs to find approximate solutions to 3 - 2^(-x) = 0.
Population growth: The population of Knoxville is 500,000 and is increasing at 3.75% per year. Approximately when will the population reach 1 million?
Population of Silver Run was 6250 in 1890, growing at 2.75% per year. Estimate population in 1915 and 1940, and when it reached 50,000.
Radioactive decay: The half-life of phosphorus-32 is about 14 days. There are 6.6 grams present initially. Express the amount remaining as a function of time t, and find when 1 gram remains.
questions.answer_hint: y(t) = 6.6 * e^(-ln2/14 * t); set y = 1 and solve for t.

questions.text: Jean invests $2300 at 6% interest compounded annually. How long until the balance reaches $4150?
Determine how much time is required for an investment to double at 6.25% compounded annually.
Determine how much time is required for an investment to triple at 5.75% compounded continuously.
A colony of bacteria starts with 1 bacterium and doubles every half hour. How many bacteria after 24 hours?
questions.answer_hint: Number of doublings = 48; total = 2^48.

questions.text: Suppose the number of disease cases is reduced by 20% each year from 10,000. How many years to reduce to 1000? To less than 1?

concepts.name: One-to-One Functions
concepts.description: A function is one-to-one if distinct inputs always produce distinct outputs. One-to-one functions pass the horizontal line test and are the only functions that can have inverses.
key_formulas: f(x1) ≠ f(x2) whenever x1 ≠ x2 (definition of one-to-one)

questions.text: Is f(x) = sqrt(x) one-to-one on its natural domain? Explain.
questions.answer_hint: Yes, because sqrt(x1) ≠ sqrt(x2) whenever x1 ≠ x2 for nonneg x.

questions.text: Is g(x) = sin x one-to-one on [0, pi]? On [0, pi/2]?
questions.answer_hint: Not on [0,pi] (sin(pi/6)=sin(5pi/6)); yes on [0,pi/2] where it is increasing.

concepts.name: Inverse Functions
concepts.description: The inverse function f^(-1) of a one-to-one function f reverses the mapping: f^(-1)(b) = a if f(a) = b. The domain and range of f and f^(-1) are interchanged. The graph of f^(-1) is the reflection of f across y = x.
key_formulas: (f^(-1) ∘ f)(x) = x for all x in domain of f
(f ∘ f^(-1))(y) = y for all y in domain of f^(-1)

questions.text: Given a one-to-one function y = f(x) by a table of values, construct the table for f^(-1)(y).
questions.answer_hint: Interchange the x and f(x) columns in the table.

questions.text: Explain why only a one-to-one function can have an inverse.
questions.answer_hint: If f(x1) = f(x2) = y for x1 ≠ x2, then f^(-1)(y) cannot be uniquely defined.

questions.text: Describe how to find the graph of f^(-1) from the graph of f.
questions.answer_hint: Reflect the graph of f across the line y = x.

sections.title: Chapter 1 Review: Questions to Guide Your Review

concepts.name: Chapter 1 Review Questions
concepts.description: Comprehensive review questions covering all major topics of Chapter 1: functions, graphs, transformations, trigonometry, exponentials, logarithms, and inverse functions.

questions.text: What is a function? What is its domain? Its range? What is an arrow diagram for a function? Give examples.
questions.answer_hint: A function assigns exactly one output to each input; domain is the set of inputs, range is the set of outputs.

questions.text: What is the graph of a real-valued function of a real variable? What is the vertical line test?
questions.answer_hint: The graph is the set of points (x, f(x)); vertical line test: each vertical line meets the graph at most once.

questions.text: What is a piecewise-defined function? Give examples.
questions.answer_hint: A function defined by different formulas on different parts of its domain.

questions.text: What are the important types of functions frequently encountered in calculus? Give an example of each type.
questions.answer_hint: Polynomial, rational, algebraic, trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic.

questions.text: What is meant by an increasing function? A decreasing function? Give an example of each.
questions.answer_hint: Increasing: f(x₁) < f(x₂) when x₁ < x₂; decreasing: f(x₁) > f(x₂) when x₁ < x₂.

questions.text: What is an even function? An odd function? What symmetry properties do their graphs have?
questions.answer_hint: Even: f(-x) = f(x), symmetric about y-axis; Odd: f(-x) = -f(x), symmetric about origin.

questions.text: If f and g are real-valued functions, how are the domains of f+g, f-g, fg, and f/g related to the domains of f and g?
questions.answer_hint: Domain of combinations is intersection of domains of f and g (excluding zeros of g for f/g).

questions.text: When is it possible to compose one function with another? Does the order of composition matter?
questions.answer_hint: Range of g must lie in domain of f for f∘g; order generally matters.

questions.text: How do you change y = f(x) to shift its graph vertically or horizontally? Give examples.
questions.answer_hint: Vertical: y = f(x) ± k; Horizontal: y = f(x ± h).

questions.text: How do you compress or stretch the graph of y = f(x) by a factor c > 1? Reflect across a coordinate axis?
questions.answer_hint: Vertical stretch: y = cf(x); horizontal compression: y = f(cx); reflections: y = -f(x) or y = f(-x).

questions.text: What is radian measure? How do you convert between radians and degrees?
questions.answer_hint: Radians = degrees × π/180; degrees = radians × 180/π.

questions.text: Graph the six basic trigonometric functions. What symmetries do the graphs have?
questions.answer_hint: sin and tan are odd; cos is even; others derived from these.

questions.text: What is a periodic function? Give examples. What are the periods of the six basic trigonometric functions?
questions.answer_hint: sin, cos, csc, sec have period 2π; tan, cot have period π.

questions.text: Starting with sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 and the formulas for cos(A+B) and sin(A+B), show how other trigonometric identities may be derived.
questions.answer_hint: Derive double angle, half angle, and sum-to-product formulas.

questions.text: How does the formula f(x) = A sin((2π/B)(x-C)) + D relate to shifting, stretching, compressing, and reflecting the graph of y = sin x?
questions.answer_hint: A = amplitude, B = period, C = horizontal shift, D = vertical shift.
